coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of carbon dots from Tagetes erecta as a precursor for determination of chlorpyrifos via fluorescence turn-off and quinalphos via fluorescence turn-on mechanisms
coredata.dc:creator: Ghosh, Subhadeep
Gul, Anam Rana
Park, Chan Yeong
Kim, Min Woo
Xu, Ping
Baek, Seung Hoon
Bhamore, Jigna R.
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
Park, Tae Jung
coredata.dc:description: Convenient one-pot synthetic route for the fabrication of carbon dots (CDs) from Tagetes erecta flower (TEF), named as “TEF-CDs’, through solvo(hydro)-thermal carbonization of the TEF was developed. The TEF-CDs revealed high selectivity towards chlorpyrifos and quinalphos, acting as a fluorescent probe. The CDs synthesized from T. erecta flower showed a strong blue color at 495 nm when excited at 420 nm, along with the exhibition of a strong quantum yield of 63.7%. The synthesized CDs revealed their richness in the surface-active organic group that synthesized CDs from T. erecta flower are mainly comprised of C, O, and N, which were crystalline in structure that was revealed by TEM image and XRD spectra. Furthermore, when the probe was exposed to different pH conditions, no major noticeable changes were recorded. Moreover, when the probe was exposed to chlorpyrifos and quinalphos, we have noticed that fluorescence spectra was turned off when the probe was exposed to chlorpyrifos and “turned on” after the exposure quinalphos. Moreover, fluorescence spectral changes showed a good linearity with chlorpyrifos and quinalphos concentrations in the range of 0.05–100.0 μM for chlorpyrifos and 0.01–50.0 μM for quinalphos. The limit of detection are 2.1 ng mL−1 and 1.7 ng mL−1 for chlorpyrifos and quinalphos, respectively. Finally, the TEF-CDs-based fluorescent nanoprobe was successfully applied to estimate chlorpyrifos and quinalphos with an effective accuracy in rice and fruit samples with rapid detection time.

XPS spectra of CDs. (a) Survey spectrum. (b) High-resolution spectra of C1s, (c) High-resolution spectra of O1s, (d) High-resolution spectra of N1s. (e) XRD spectra of CD synthesized from T. erecta flower.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of TEF-CDs with different pesticides (dichlorvos, hexaconazole, monocrotophos, acephate, indoxacarb, glyphosate, thiamethoxam, triazophos, imidacloprid, propiconazole, tebuconazole, chlorpyrifos, and quinalphos at 1 mM concentration (λ ex: 420 nm, λ em: 495 nm). (a) Fluorescence spectra of TEF-CDs with the addition of different concentrations of quinalphos from (1000 μM–0.01 μM) (λ ex: 420 nm, λ em: 495 nm). (b) Calibration curve fabricated between I/Io and various concentrations of quinalphos (0.01 μM–50 μM).
(a) Hydrolysis of quinalphos and its binding mechanism with TEF-CDs. (b) Binding mechanism of chlorpyrifos with TEF-CDs through (H-bonding).

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of carbon dots from Tagetes erecta as a precursor for determination of chlorpyrifos via fluorescence turn-off and quinalphos via fluorescence turn-on mechanisms

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Convenient one-pot synthetic route for the fabrication of carbon dots (CDs) from Tagetes erecta flower (TEF), named as “TEF-CDs’, through solvo(hydro)-thermal carbonization of the TEF was developed. The TEF-CDs revealed high selectivity towards chlorpyrifos and quinalphos, acting as a fluorescent probe. The CDs synthesized from T. erecta flower showed a strong blue color at 495 nm when excited at 420 nm, along with the exhibition of a strong quantum yield of 63.7%. The synthesized CDs revealed their richness in the surface-active organic group that synthesized CDs from T. erecta flower are mainly comprised of C, O, and N, which were crystalline in structure that was revealed by TEM image and XRD spectra. Furthermore, when the probe was exposed to different pH conditions, no major noticeable changes were recorded. Moreover, when the probe was exposed to chlorpyrifos and quinalphos, we have noticed that fluorescence spectra was turned off when the probe was exposed to chlorpyrifos and “turned on” after the exposure quinalphos. Moreover, fluorescence spectral changes showed a good linearity with chlorpyrifos and quinalphos concentrations in the range of 0.05–100.0 μM for chlorpyrifos and 0.01–50.0 μM for quinalphos. The limit of detection are 2.1 ng mL−1 and 1.7 ng mL−1 for chlorpyrifos and quinalphos, respectively. Finally, the TEF-CDs-based fluorescent nanoprobe was successfully applied to estimate chlorpyrifos and quinalphos with an effective accuracy in rice and fruit samples with rapid detection time.

Carbon dots were synthesized from Tagetes erecta flower content with solvo(hydro)thermal procedure which further emits blue-color emission under UV light at 365 nm. Further quenching was observed upon the addition of chlorpyrifos, and signal enhancement in fluorescence spectra was recorded upon the addition of quinalphos.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials with special properties within confined diameter have tremendously gained wide popularity in various fields of science and technology over the past two decades especially carbon-based materials, carbon dots (CDs), which was discovered by Xu et al. in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004). CDs have been highlighted among researchers owing to their interesting properties, such as light emission in varying colors, outstanding light stability with resistance in photobleaching (Jiang et al., 2015), low toxicity (Xia et al., 2019), biocompatibility with small size and molecular weight (Wang et al., 2011), simple preparation process (Roy et al., 2015), surface-active organic groups that are hydrophilic in nature (Kong et al., 2012), bright-color photoluminescence (Sun et al., 2006), quasi-spherical luminescent nanoparticles with amorphous and crystalline cores (Zhao et al., 2015), high-temperature resistance (Mohammed and Omer, 2020), and multiphoton upconversion (Yuan et al., 2016). In spite of all these major advantages of CDs, they are limited by low quantum yield (QY) in the red region spectrum (Hola et al., 2014). Several studies have been conducted with the aim of minimizing this unfavorable feature (Wang et al., 2010; Guo et al., 2012). Given the extensive attractive properties of CDs, the fabricated CDs have been used as outstanding candidates for numerous applications such as thermal sensing (Kalytchuk et al., 2017), bioimaging(Bogireddy et al., 2020), drug delivery (Wang et al., 2013; Hola et al., 2014; Yuan et al., 2016), biolabeling (Sun et al., 2013a), chemical sensing (Dong et al., 2012; Bogireddy et al., 2020), sensing of heavy metal ions (Zhang and Chen, 2014; Gao et al., 2016; Bhamore et al., 2018; Kailasa et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020), photodynamic therapy (Ge et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2020), photothermal therapy (Ardekani et al., 2017; Geng et al., 2020) and enhancement in solar cells (Zhu et al., 2014), recovery of oil samples (Baragau et al., 2020) certainly stand out. Furthermore, the CDs have been synthesized from different plants and plant derivatives for different purposes, such as Ginkgo biloba leaves (Li et al., 2020) was used for the detection of pesticides, Saccharum officinarum juice (Mehta et al., 2014) was used for fluorescent imaging of bacteria and yeast, apple juice (Mehta et al., 2015) was used to image Mycobacterium and fungal cells, cauliflower leaves (Dinh and Gomes, 2020) used for water splitting, mung bean sprouts (Wang et al., 2018) to check the growth and photosynthesis, Magnolia liliiflora flower (Atchudan et al., 2018) was used to detect Fe3+ ion and cellular multicolor imaging with a quantum yield (QY) of 11% with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.2 μM, Osmanthus fragrans flower (Wang et al., 2019) used to detect Fe3+, ascorbic acid and cellular imaging their QY of the synthesized carbon dot is around and LOD was 5 μM, and Borassus flabellifer flower (Murugan and Sundramoorthy, 2018) was used to detect Fe3+ ion with QY of 11.73% and LOD achieved was 10 nM. Previously, CDs synthesized either doped or undoped was used as a probe for the detection of various pesticide like “sucrose mediated CDs was used to detect paraoxon-ethyl pesticide” (Chang et al., 2017), “N-doped CQDs was used to detect atrazine” (Mohapatra et al., 2018), “N,P doped CDs was used for the detection of carbendazim” (Yang et al., 2018), Cauliflower based carbon dot was used to detect diazinon, glyphosate and amicarbazone (Ashrafi Tafreshi et al., 2020) but previously flower-based fluorescent method confines in their higher detection limit as well as the enhancement in the QY and on-time rapid detection, with simplicity in sample preparation, thus, in order to overcome these barrier we have focused on the synthesis of TEF-CDs from the above-mentioned precursors which showed unique optical and surface chemistry, that allows tuning their applications to specific molecules or targets. In order to synthesize CDs with a new precursor, Tagetes erecta flower was selected as a unique source for the preparation of CDs, which is termed as “T. erecta flower carbon dots (TEF-CDs)” apart from specifically targeting pesticide molecules like chlorpyrifos and quinalphos. The fluorescence-based nano-probe provides on-time rapid estimation with effective accuracy achieving a much lower detection limit with a moderately high quantum yield.

In the modern phase, pesticide dependency has become more reliable for farmers in order to protect their crops from pests and diseases to ensure a high yield of agricultural commodities, despite the severe damages they cause potential toxicity and risk to human health even in small concentrations (Jin et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2020). Among these, chlorpyrifos and quinalphos belong to those organophosphorus pesticides that have been widely used on various crops. Thus, the consumption of chlorpyrifos directly or indirectly causes several toxic effects including paresthesia, light-headedness, tachycardia, cranial nerve palsies, weakness in facial and proximal limb muscles, and neck and partial respiratory paralysis (Rathod and Garg, 2017). Similarly, quinalphos appears with several health hazards like pancreatitis, hypernatremia, renal failure (Viswanathan, 2013), weakness, fatigue causing low scoring memory, and learning ability (Lockridge et al., 2015). A number of studies stated that chlorpyrifos-oxon was formed in the body when chlorpyrifos is exposed to the liver (Park et al., 2009), yielding to cause a respirational poison, which is 1000 times more toxic than chlorpyrifos (Srivastava et al., 2011). Therefore, it is crucial to detect these kinds of harmful pesticides at a low level of concentration. To estimate the trace level amount of both pesticides, highly selective, facile, accurate, and sensitive analytical methods are essentially required. However, they required either specific solvents or sample preparation, and especially are sophisticated instruments. In order to establish facile analytical method, various nanomaterials have been integrated with spectroscopic techniques for the detection of several pesticides, such as carbendazim (Patel et al., 2015), ethyl parathion (Bala et al., 2015), triazophos (Bhamore et al., 2016), carbaryl (Sun et al., 2013b), fenvalerate, dithianon, dinoseb (Li et al., 2020), and dichlorvos (D’souza et al., 2014). In order to develop a green analytical method, the carbon dots are prepared via the hydrothermal method using TEF as a raw material and studied their analytical applications for assaying of pesticides with different mechanisms.

The T. erecta flower is commonly known as a marigold flower. Traditionally, it was used for medical treatment against muscular pain, ulcers, piles, and kidney problems (Priyanka et al., 2013). However, nowadays, T. erecta exhibits extensive pharmacological applicability including antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidant, anti-diabetic, anti-fungal, and anticancer activities (Singh et al., 2020). In contrast to the aforementioned health hazards of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos, we fabricated a fluorescent-based sensing platform using the TEF as a herald for the synthesis of CDs via the solvo(hydro) thermal technique. The as-synthesized TEF-CDs were used as a fluorescent sensor for the rapid estimation of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos, which were stable for at least 42 days without making any major change or shifts in the spectra (Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information). Additionally, the TEF-CDs emit blue light under the UV lamp with excitation at 365 nm. Meanwhile, the synthesized TEF-CDs exhibit a strong inclination toward chlorpyrifos and quinalphos when subjected to the turn-on and turn-off mechanism, respectively. It also exhibits favorable selectivity when exposed to different pesticides and metal ions. This method established a wide linear range concentration for chlorpyrifos (0.05–100 μM) (1.7 × 101–3.5 × 105 ng mL−1) and quinalphos (0.01–50 μM) (2.90–2.98 × 105 ng mL−1) with a respective limit of detection (LOD) of 2.1 ng mL−1 and 1.7 ng mL−1, respectively. Increased sensitivity of the synthesized TEF-CDs is mainly due to the presence of a critical surface group that ensured the selectivity towards two different pesticides, chlorpyrifos and quinalphos, with simplicity in the sample preparation and rapid detection time without any further modifications or alterations in the synthesized TEF-CDs with moderately high QY (63.7%) with relatively low detection limit was achieved. Further interference study provided the details that the probe is hardly selective with other pesticides, biofluids or metal ions. Finally, this probe was effectively utilized for the determination of two pesticides in rice and fruit samples with effective accuracy.

T. erecta (African marigold) flower was collected from the local market outside the campus of SVNIT (Surat, India). All chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade and used without any further purification. NiSO4·6H2O, MnSO4, Fe(OH)3, K2Cr2O7, Mg(NO3)3·6H2O, Cu(SO4)·5H2O, ZnSO4, KBr, Pb(NO3)2, KCl, Hg(NO3)2·H2O, NaCl, FeCl3, Co(NO3)·6H2O, methanol, AlCl3, histidine, cysteine, and glutathione were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Dichlorvos, hexaconazole, monocrotophos, and acephate were obtained from Super Crop Safe (Ahmedabad, India). Chlorpyrifos, indoxacarb, and quinalphos were purchased from Gharda Chemical (Mumbai, India). Glyphosate and thiamethoxam were obtained from United Phosphorous (Mumbai, India). Triazophos was obtained from Gujarat Insecticides (Ankleshwar, India). Imidacloprid, propiconazole, and tebuconazole were obtained from Atul (Ankleshwar, India). Glycine was received from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA), and l(+)-ascorbic acid was received from Samchun Chemical (Pyeongtaek, Korea). Ultrapure water was obtained from Milli-Q purification (Millipore, MA, USA).

The as-synthesized TEF-CDs from T. erecta were characterized using various analytical instruments. UV–Visible absorption spectra were recorded using the Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, Largo, FL, USA). The Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to measure the fluorescence spectra (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA). The surface charge of TEF-CDs was measured using zeta potential and the particle size analyzer (Otsuka ELSZ-1000, Otsuka Electronics, Osaka, Japan). Surface functionalities were measured by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (FTIR APHA II, Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to reveal the surface composition of the synthesized TEF-CDs using the source type AI K Alpha (Thermo Fisher Scientific, K Alpha+, Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was used for phase identification using XRD analyzer (New D8-Advance, Brucker AXS, Berlin, Germany). Lastly, morphological evaluation and the size of bare TEF-CDs were determined using the field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, JEM-F200, Jeol, Tokyo, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence decay (TRF) decay using a confocal microscope (Micro Time-200, Pico quant, Germany), a single-mode pulsed diode laser (375 nm with 30 ps pulse width and average power of ∼40 nW) was used as an excitation source, and a time-correlated single-photon counting system (PicoHarp300, Pico Quant GmbH, Germany) was used to count emission photons. A dichroic mirror (Z375RDC, AHF), a long pass filter (HQ405lp, AHF), and an avalanche photodiode detector (PDM series, MPD) were used to collect emission photons from the samples.

The green approach was used for the synthesis of TEF-CDs, which emitted a strong blue light-excretion at 365 nm. Firstly, fresh T. erecta flowers were collected from the outside of SVNIT, Surat, India. The disk flowers were separated from the dark green foliage and then washed with deionized (DI) water to remove any dust particles. Thereafter, approximately 10 g of flowers was weighed and crushed in a motor and pestle. The crude was then mixed with 60 mL of DI water and stored for ultrasonication for approximately 40 min for the extraction of multiple phytochemical compounds from the crude. Next, the solution was filtered to remove the large particles and was finally transferred into a Teflon lined steel autoclave for 5 h at 200 °C. After the temperature of the reaction was lowered to room temperature (25 °C–30 °C), the solution was filtered using a Whatman filter paper (Grade 292; 125 mm in diameter) to eliminate the heavier particles and dialyzed (Dialysis membrane 70; 17.5 mm in diameter) for 6 h followed by the centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the heavier particles and other unreacted particles to further obtain ultra-small TEF-CDs. This solution was stored at 4 °C for further use. A schematic representation for the synthesis of TEF-CDs is shown in Scheme 1 .

In a typical experiment, chlorpyrifos and quinalphos were detected via fluorescence spectroscopy. Firstly, stock solutions of chlorpyrifos (1.0 mM) and quinalphos (1.0 mM) were prepared. Thereafter, the stockpiled TEF-CDs of 1.0 mL was taken into a couple of 4.0 mL glass vial separately. Next, 1.0 mM solutions of 0.5 mL chlorpyrifos and quinalphos were added to the glass vials and then vortexed for 1 min and left to stand still for 3 min before proceeding for fluorescence spectrum (λ ex at 420 nm, λ em at 495 nm) measurement. The changes in fluorescence intensity and different concentrations of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos were measured via the construction of a calibration graph. Thereafter, to investigate the selectivity of the probe towards chlorpyrifos and quinalphos, potentially interfering pesticides such as monocrotophos, triazophos, glyphosate, indoxacarb, and acephate were tested.

The QY of TEF-CDs was calculated using the standard guideline reference sample (quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4) with the emission intensity at 495 nm. The QY of the TEF-CDs was found to be 63.7% using the standard equation as follows:

where the “Q” represents the QY of TEF-CDs, and QQS denotes the QY of quinine sulphate, the CD subscript indicates carbon dots, “R” which is the subscript of I indicates the reference, “I” is the measured integrated fluorescence emission intensity, “A” is the UV-absorbance. Furthermore, to restrain the re-absorption, UV-absorption was maintained below 1, “η” is implied to be “refractive index” of the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos recovery in spiked samples
ce:section.ce:para: The as-synthesized TEF-CDs were analyzed using the spiked samples in fruits (apple, strawberry, and lemon) and rice. Apple, strawberry, lemon, and rice were brought from the nearby marketplace and used without additional treatment. These fresh fruits were ground and homogenized using a blender and were then filtered using the Whatman filter (Grade 292) paper to remove the bigger fragments. Further, the samples were diluted with DI water to 10 folds. Next, the diluted samples were spiked using different concentrations of chlorpyrifos (0.5, 0.1, 0.05 μM) and quinalphos (0.1, 0.05, 0.01 μM) and allowed to stand for 1 h. Furthermore, 10 mL of methanol was added to the vortex for 8 min to form a uniform concentration. Later on, 3.0 g of sodium sulphate was added and filtered through a 0.45-μm syringe filter. Finally, the solvent was evaporated towards obtaining a concentrated solution, and the concentration of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos was estimated using fluorescence spectroscopy.

According to the previous procedure (Rohit and Kailasa, 2014) with some minor modifications, chlorpyrifos in the rice sample was prepared to analyze the amount. Firstly, 5.0 g of polished rice sample was finely ground into a powder and spiked with chlorpyrifos (1.0 μg/mL) and then kept stand for 24 h. The pre-treated rice sample was wrapped in filter paper and laid on a Soxhlet extractor soaked in 60 mL of methanol for 14 h. Thereafter, 100 mL of methanol was added to a round bottom flask. Chlorpyrifos was extracted by heating the mantle at 75 °C for 6 h. Finally, the extracted solution was concentrated to 3.0 mL, and the resultant solution was used for the fluorescence estimation of chlorpyrifos using TEF-CDs.

Spiked sample analysis of quinalphos in rice was performed through the previous procedure (Rohit et al., 2016). Firstly, 5.0 g of rice sample was enriched with various concentrations (0.1, 0.05, 0.01 μM) of quinalphos and kept standing for 1 day. Thereafter, quinalphos was extracted by adding 12 mL of methanol, followed by ultrasonication for 25 min and decanting. This procedure was repeated several times and then filtered through a 0.45 μM syringe filter. Finally, the methanol was evaporated, and the collected residue was diluted with DI water. The amount of quinalphos was estimated by fluorescence using TEF-CDs.

The as-synthesis of TEF-CDs are yellowish in daylight and show a bright blue fluorescence under the illumination of UV at 365 nm (Fig. 1 ). In order to generate TEF-CDs with high fluorescence intensity, TEF-CDs were synthesized at four different temperature 100 °C, 150 °C, 200 °C, and 250 °C (Fig. S2), showing high emission intensity at hydrothermal temperature 200 °C, which represents that 200 °C is the optimum hydrothermal temperature for TEF-CDs and also to maximize the efficiency of TEF-CDs we went through four different time interval studies for 3, 4, 5, and 6 h, further which exposes that 5 h emits the maximum emission intensity (Fig. S2a-b). Furthermore, to predict the maximum emission peak of TEF-CDs, the fluorescence spectrum was measured at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 400 nm to 490 nm, revealing its maximum emission peak was observed at 495 nm when excited at 420 nm (Fig. S3). Furthermore, a confirmatory test was performed using fluorescence spectroscopy with the highest emission being at 495 nm and maximum excitation at 420 nm (Fig. 1). After that, the surface charge of the TEF-CDs was measured using zeta potential analysis and found that the TEF-CDs bears a negative charge of −14.91 mV. The addition of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos into the TEF-CDs exhibited aggregation, which further changed the surface charge from negative (−14.91 mV) to positive (+20.23 and + 26.58 mV) (Fig. S8a). The lifetime of TEF-CDs was measured to be 2.59 ns (Fig. S8b). The surface functional groups of the synthesized TEF-CDs were analyzed through FTIR spectroscopy and compared with the spectra of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos (Figs S4 and S5). The TEF-CDs yielded strong characteristic peaks within the range of 3000–3500 cm−1, thus establishing the presence of N–H and –OH stretching vibrations. The weak absorption peak at 2920 cm−1 represented the stretching vibrations of C–H, and the medium absorption peak at 1635 cm−1 indicated CO stretching vibrations of the aliphatic ketone. However, the medium peak at approximately 1350–1450 cm−1 represented C–H bending vibrations, and that at approximately 1050–1120 cm−1 contributed to the C–N stretching vibrations of amine. Finally, the TEF-CDs yielded a strong absorption peak at 320 nm in UV–visible spectroscopy, which implies that it was spawned by the CO from n→π∗ transitions.

The average percentage of elemental composition and electronic configuration of the synthesized TEF-CDs were determined via XPS analysis (Fig. 2 a–d), whereby the survey spectrum of the synthesized TEF-CDs (Fig. 2a) consisted of three different peaks at 284.84 eV, 399.79 eV, and 532.43 eV belong to the elements of C1s, N1s, and O1s, which confirms the presence of C, N, and O, respectively. Furthermore, the elemental percentage composition of TEF-CDs mainly comprised of carbon (69.92%), oxygen (28.04%), and nitrogen (2.03%). The high-resolution XPS spectra of C, N, and O revealed that the binding energy at 284.84, 399.79, and 532.43 eV are attributed due to the CC, C–OH, and C–O–C (1°/2° amines of Ns) bonds, respectively. The FT-IR and XPS data demonstrate that the synthesized TEF-CDs have active surface functional groups.

The crystalline or amorphous phase structures of the TEF-CDs were studied using the XRD analyzer (Fig. 2e). The peak at 002 establishes that the synthesized TEF-CDs are crystalline in nature. The peak at 28.38° represents the crystal plane of graphite (sp
2 ) nature of the TEF-CDs. The d-spacing for the peak at 002 was found to be 3.10 Å, which was similar to that of the graphite lattice plane. The size and morphology of the synthesized TEF-CDs were measured via a field emission-transmission electron microscopy as shown in Fig. 3 a–b. The results indicate that the synthesized TEF-CDs delicately diffused in an aqueous medium with a diameter of 3.41 ± 0.53 nm, confirming the histogram and the calculation of d-spacing finds out to be 0.170 nm, which reveals that the TEF-CDs are well agreement with graphitic lattice plane. The stability of TEF-CDs was studied by measuring the fluorescence spectra at different time intervals (1–42 days). The stability of the material through the intensity graph was depicted that the nanomaterials tend to aggregate through the interval of times, and in fact, the material starts to fade its fluorescence (Fig. S1a). These spectral data revealed that there was no significant change in the fluorescence spectra of TEF-CDs, suggesting the TEF-CDs are stable and could be used as a probe for sensing pesticides. Furthermore, the FE-TEM image of TEF-CDs with the addition of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos discloses the aggregation (Fig. S7). Taking into consideration of photostability of the synthesized carbon dots, the dialyzed solution of the as-prepared TEF-CDs was further kept under UV irradiation at 365 nm and measured its stability for around 9 h. It was worth noticeable that TEF-CDs begin to lose their fluorescence over the period of time under the long exposure of UV irradiation (Fig. S1b). In order to stabilize the sample fluorescence intensity, we choose to store in dark condition at 4οC.

To enhance the sensing capability of the synthesized TEF-CDs toward pesticides, the effect of pH was carefully checked on the basis of the fluorescence quenching mechanisms with chlorpyrifos and enhancing mechanism with quinalphos, using phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) with a wide range of pH (2.0–12.0). The spectra of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos revealed that the solution had no effect with or without the presence of pesticides. Therefore, the determination of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos was performed without any buffered pH (Fig. S6a-c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the sensing applications towards pesticides, the aqueous dispersion of TEF-CDs was treated with a large number of other pesticides (dichlorvos, hexaconazole, monocrotophos, acephate, indoxacarb, glyphosate, thiamethoxam, triazophos, imidacloprid, propiconazole, and tebuconazole; 1.0 mM) (Fig. 4 a), and reducing molecules that could possibly interfere in the real samples like histidine, cysteine and glutathione, glycine, and l(+)-ascorbic acid (1.0 mM) (Fig. 4b). Furthermore, the probe showed a remarkable response by the addition of chlorpyrifos (fluorescence quenching) and quinalphos (fluorescence enhancement). However, the effect of enhancing or quenching was not exhibited by other pesticides and possibly interfering with reducing molecules; thus, the aforementioned observation clearly indicates that the probe acts as a fluorescence detector for both chlorpyrifos and quinalphos pesticides. Upon further observation, the fluorescence emission intensity of the synthesized TEF-CDs was evaluated against various concentrations (1000 μM–0.05 μM) of chlorpyrifos, as shown in Fig. 5 a. The fluorescence emission intensity of the TEF-CDs (I0/I) increased in rationally, which was proportional to the concentration level of chlorpyrifos. Furthermore, a calibration graph of the emission intensity (I0/I) values and the concentrations (0.05 μM–1000 μM) of chlorpyrifos was plotted. The graph displayed decent linearity with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9864, where I0 and I represented the emission intensity with and without chlorpyrifos, respectively (Fig. 5b). Finally, the limit of detection (LOD) of TEF-CDs with chlorpyrifos was found to be 2.1 ng mL−1. Thereafter, the increasing concentration of quinalphos was found to act as a turn on emission intensity at 495 nm (Fig. 6 a). The emission intensity of the prepared probe was measured with various concentrations (1000 μM–0.01 μM) of quinalphos as shown in Fig. 6a. The emission intensity of the TEF-CDs (I/I0) decreased consistently proportional to the concentration level of quinalphos. Thereafter, a calibration graph of emission intensity (I/I0) values against the concentrations (0.01 μM–1000 μM) of quinalphos were plotted. The graph displayed decent linearity, yielding a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9965, where I and I0 represented the emission intensity without and with the presence of quinalphos, respectively (Fig. 6b). Furthermore, the LOD of the TEF-CDs with quinalphos was found to be 1.7 ng mL−1. This fluorescence-based analytical method was compared against other analytical methods, revealing that the CD-based fluorescence method exhibited significantly lower LOD than other methodological pathways as shown in Tables 1 and 2
.

To take a deep dive into the CD-based fluorescence-based sensor, determining the fluorescence quenching mechanism sets off the key parameter for decay dynamics. Mainly two types of quenching dominate the fluorescence quenching mechanism where we can describe those mechanisms as static quenching and dynamic (collisional) quenching. Static quenching involves complex formation between the quencher and the fluorophore. This complex formation is non-fluorescent. Dynamic quenching affects the lifetime of the excited states in which the fluorophore further diffuses in its lifetime in this quenching process. There does not bring any tenacious difference to the molecules, but each quenching process needs fluorophore and the quencher in contact with each other. Thus, in order to understand the decay dynamics by time-corelated single-photon counting analysis, it is necessary to understand the TEF-CDs quenching activated by chlorpyrifos pesticide. The synthesized TEF-CDs have a lifetime of 2.59 ns (Fig. S8a), but when exposed to the selected pesticide, chlorpyrifos, its lifetime decreased from 2.59 ns to 2.52 ns (Fig. S8b). The slight decrease of a lifetime is due to the complex formation between chlorpyrifos and TEF-CDs and through Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
k q
τ 0
[ Q ]
=
τ 0
/ τ
where F0 and F are the fluorescence emission intensities of TEF-CDs, kq is the biomolecular quenching constant, τ0 (2.59 ns) is the lifetime of the TEF-CDs and τ (2.52 ns) is the lifetime of the TEF-CDs with the presence of pesticides, “Q’ is the concentration of pesticides used in the experiment, after the calculation of the Stern-Volmer equation we found that the quenching mechanism of chlorpyrifos with TEF-CDs mainly deals with the static quenching mechanism within the concentration region of 0.05–100 μM. Furthermore, the lifetime of TEF-CDs with quinalphos, which reveals that the lifetime to be 2.62 ns (Fig. S8c). It further gives an insight view that fluorescence enhancing occurred due to the binding with the phosphate group of quinalphos with the C–N group of TEF-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of sensing chlorpyrifos and quinalphos
ce:section.ce:para: The as-synthesized TEF-CDs from plant or plant derivatives contain active organic groups such as C–N, –NH, and –OH, which further act as a precursor for binding. The binding mechanism chlorpyrifos with TEF-CDs can be defined using the obtained FTIR spectra, because as earlier mentioned, the active surface organic group plays a crucial role in the binding with the pesticides. In the FTIR spectra, we speculate that the peak reduces in the –OH peak, which further suggests the formation of hydrogen bonding in TEF-CDs that traps surface energy results in fluorescence quenching (Fig. 7 ) and further aggregation with TEF-CDs in the presence of chlorpyrifos (Figs S4 and S7a-b). Similarly, where the vanishing of C–N stretching vibrations indicated a binding between the C–N group of TEF-CDs and the phosphate group of quinalphos (Figs. 7 and S5). The binding mechanism through the electrostatic interaction between quinalphos and the nitro group of CDs was found (Gupta et al., 2012; Rohit et al., 2016). To further go insight, view the reactive amino group of TEF-CDs forms the electrostatic interaction with quinalphos breaking the formation of the complex between inter TEF-CDs, which led them to enhance in the fluorescence enhancement. FE-TEM images of TEF-CDs with chlorpyrifos and quinalphos are shown in Fig. S7. These images revealed that the size and morphology of TEF-CDs were drastically changed with the addition of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos, suggesting the aggregation of TEF-CDs induced by both pesticides via non-covalent interactions. Furthermore, zeta potentials of TEF-CDs were measured with the addition of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos (Fig. S8d), representing the zeta potential of pure TEF-CDs was changed from negative (−14.91 mV) to positive values + 26.58 and + 20.23 mV, respectively, which confirms the interactions between TEF-CDs and both pesticides. In order to put this into a nutshell, the binding mechanism between TEF-CDs and chlorpyrifos takes place due to the H-bonding between the phosphate group of the chlorpyrifos and carbonyl (CO) group of TEF-CDs which is responsible for bond formation following the aggregation resulting in fluorescence quenching; Further evidence that satisfied the resultant explanation is lifetime analysis where the lifetime decreased to 2.52 ns with the addition of chlorpyrifos, and when quinalphos was introduced into the solution of TEF-CDs the amine group gets activated forming an electrostatic interaction between amine groups of TEF-CDs and hydrolysed compound of quinalphos which further transfer the Förster resonance electron transfer (FRET) the energy to quinalphos which resulted in the enhancement of the fluorescence intensity further the lifetime enhancement was noticed to 2.62 ns with the addition of quinalphos.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of TEF-CDs towards chlorpyrifos and quinalphos
ce:section.ce:para: Even though the selectivity of TEF-CDs towards chlorpyrifos and quinalphos in the presence of other possible pesticides (dichlorvos, hexaconazole, monocrotophos, acephate, indoxacarb, glyphosate, thiamethoxam, triazophos, imidacloprid, propiconazole, and tebuconazole; 1.0 mM), it was noticed that the addition of other pesticides into TEF-CDs did not show either fluorescence enhancement or quenching. However, the additions of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos were found to cause selective fluorescence quenching and enhancement, respectively, in the presence of other possible pesticides. The spectral data shows that the quinalphos and chlorpyrifos exhibited high selectivity in the presence of other pesticides. Any other potential interfering agents in the fluorescence estimation for chlorpyrifos and quinalphos were checked using different inorganic metal ions (cations: Ni2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr6+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Na+, Co2+, Zn2+, and Hg2+), and different anions (Cl−, SO4 2−, Br−, NO3 −, PO4 3−, and S2−). Additionally, to check interference with alternative pesticides, the TEF-CDs were incumbent with the possible pesticides, such as triazophos, monocrotophos, glyphosate, acephate, indoxacarb, and dichlorvos (1.0 mM) during the sensing of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos, as the above types of pesticides are often present in fruits and rice (Fig. S9). The fluorescence quenching and enhancing were only exhibited by chlorpyrifos and quinalphos, whereas the interaction of other pesticides with TEF-CDs did not induce any enhancement or quenching. Furthermore, taking into account we studied the presence of quinalphos and chlorpyrifos in the presence of one another towards the probe, we found that the presence of quinalphos in the probe enhances the signal intensity, but further addition of chlorpyrifos into the existed solution hardly affected the fluorescence intensity, and we also noticed the same in vice versa situation in the presence of chlorpyrifos that the fluorescence quenching in the probe takes place further the addition of quinalphos into the existed solution have no effect in the change of fluorescence intensity (Fig. S10a-b). From this result, we can get an insight view that the presence of quinalphos or chlorpyrifos in the probe cancels out each other. Furthermore, the binding affinity between quinalphos and chlorpyrifos was measured in order to support the statement of coexistence of quinalphos and chlorpyrifos in a single sample. After performing the analysis (Fig. S11), quinalphos has a greater tendency to bind with TEF-CDs in that condition where quinalphos and chlorpyrifos coexisted in a sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of quinalphos and chlorpyrifos in fruits and rice samples

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, the fluorescent carbon dots were fabricated from T. erecta flower as a carbon source via a hydrothermal method. The as-synthesized carbon dots from T. erecta flower (TEF-CDs) showed a strong blue emission peak at 495 nm with a high QY of 63.7%. The spectral data of FTIR and XPS demonstrated that the surfaces of T. erecta flower consist of active organic functional groups and are mainly comprised of basic elements like C, O, and N. Furthermore, the as-prepared TEF-CDs exhibited good dispersibility in an aqueous medium with a size around 3.41 ± 0.53 nm. The TEF-CDs acted as a fluorescent probe for the estimation of chlorpyrifos via fluorescence “turn-off” and quinalphos via fluorescence “turn-on” mechanisms, respectively. The developed method exhibited LODs of 2.1 ng mL−1 and 1.7 ng mL−1 for chlorpyrifos and quinalphos, respectively, which is quite lower than the reported methods. The as-prepared TEF-CDs exhibited exceptional selectivity for the determination of both pesticides and were successfully applied to detect chlorpyrifos and quinalphos in fruits and rice samples. Thus, the as-prepared CDs could be considered as a facile pesticide fluorescent sensor to establish a facile analytical platform for assaying of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos in food and environmental samples.

Subhadeep Ghosh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Examination. Anam Rana Gul: Methodology, Validation, Visualization. Chan Yeong Park: Methodology, Validation. Min Woo Kim: Methodology, Validation. Xu Ping: Methodology, Validation. Seung Hoon Baek: Methodology, Visualization. Jigna R. Bhamore: Methodology, Writing. Suresh Kumar Kailasa: Resources, Investigation, Writing. Tae Jung Park: Supervision, Writing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
